About NODE

What is NODE?

NODE is a modular robotics system designed for middle school students to learn design, engineering, and creative problem solving through building.

It combines cardboard-based construction with simple plug-and-play electronics, allowing students to design physical structures that can move, react, and behave in real time.

Everything is built to be approachable. Students don't need prior coding or engineering experience to start building meaningful projects.

Our Mission & Vision

Our mission is to make engineering and design feel accessible, creative, and hands-on for younger students.

There has traditionally been a gap between physical building, electronics, and programming. NODE is designed to bring those worlds together in a way that feels natural instead of intimidating.

We want students to stop thinking of technology as something abstract or distant. Instead, it should feel like something they can build with, experiment on, and understand by doing.

Over time, NODE is meant to grow into a system where students don't just follow instructions, but start inventing their own ideas and bringing them to life.

Why Cardboard?

Cardboard is one of the most underrated prototyping materials.

It is cheap, widely available, and easy to work with. More importantly, it lowers the pressure to get things "right" the first time.

In most engineering tools, mistakes are expensive. In NODE, mistakes are part of the process.

Students can cut, rebuild, and test quickly, which naturally encourages iteration. This is where a lot of real learning happens.

Cardboard is also often the first prototyping material for many designers and engineers. Before advanced tools, before precision manufacturing, ideas usually start in something simple and physical like cardboard.

What makes it powerful is how open-ended it is. It is not just a material, but a starting point. Students can reinforce it with tape or glue, draw directly onto it, or combine it with other materials to improve strength, structure, or expression. It becomes a base layer for thinking through ideas in 3D.

Over time, it teaches a mindset: that good design does not start perfect, it starts rough, and gets better through testing and change.

What NODE Enables

NODE is not just about building models. It is about building systems that respond to input and behave in intentional ways.

Students can design structures, connect motors, and see immediate physical results from their decisions.

As they progress, they start to move from guided builds into open-ended projects where they define both the structure and the behavior.

Explore NODE
